blocks.md:
Theek hai — yeh raha **ClickHouse** ka full story, **Learn & Be Curious** framing me (tera locked primary). Simple English, screen se padhne ke liye.

---

**Story: G1 — ClickHouse move · Learn & Be Curious · Amazon · ~3-4 min**

**Opener (yeh pehle bol):**

> "At my last job, our database was dying under 10 million writes a day. I had never used a column-based database, and I had never written Go. I got curious about why the database was failing, so I went deep and taught myself both from scratch. The one key thing I learned shaped the whole design — and it cut our query time from 30 seconds to 12, and our storage by five times."

### Situation
At Good Creator Co., we crawled lakhs of profiles a day. Every crawl wrote a log — follower count, likes, and so on — into PostgreSQL. At 10 million writes a day, the database was choking. Write time went from 5 milliseconds up to 500. Our reports took 30 seconds. And storage cost kept climbing.

### Task
I had to fix it. But here is the honest part — I did not know how. I had never used a column database, and I had never written Go. So before I could fix anything, I had to learn.

### Action
First, I got curious about the real cause. Why was a database just falling over like this? I read about row storage versus column storage. A normal database stores data row by row, so for a report it must read every row. A column database like ClickHouse stores data column by column, so it reads only the one column you ask for. That is much faster for reports.

So I picked ClickHouse. But I did not stop at "it is faster." I went deeper, and I found the key thing — ClickHouse hates small writes. Every single write makes a new file on disk, and too many small files breaks it. So I could not just point the old code at the new database. It would fail.

That one fact shaped the whole design. I built a small buffer in Go that holds 1000 events, then writes them all in one go. If 1000 pile up, it writes. If not, a one-minute timer writes whatever is there. So ClickHouse always gets big, clean writes — never small ones.

I had to learn Go for this too. I picked it because Go's channels were a clean fit for this buffer-and-flush pattern, and Go is light on memory. I gave it about two weeks of real study before I wrote any production code.

Last, I was careful. I ran a two-week dual-write — writing to both the old and new database at the same time — and checked the counts matched before I switched over. So there was no data loss.

### Result
Reports went from 30 seconds down to 12 — about 2.5 times faster. Storage went from 500 GB down to 100 — five times smaller. Infra cost dropped 30 percent. The writes to the database went from 10,000 a second down to 10 a second, because they were now in big batches.

And the best part for me — I went in knowing nothing about column databases or Go, and I came out having shipped a real system in both.

> 💬 **60-second version:**
> "Our database was dying at 10 million writes a day. I had never used a column database or Go. I got curious about why it was failing, read about column storage, and found the key thing — ClickHouse hates small writes. So I built a buffer in Go that batches 1000 events into one write. I taught myself both tools from scratch, ran a two-week dual-write to be safe, and shipped it. Query time dropped from 30 seconds to 12, and storage by five times."

### My learnings
> "Two things stayed with me. One — learn the 'why' before you build. If I had not learned that ClickHouse hates small writes, I would have shipped the old pattern and it would have failed in hours. Two — taking two weeks to learn first did not slow me down. It is what let me ship clean, with zero data loss."

### Likely follow-ups

**Q: "How long did you spend learning before you built?"**
> "About two weeks. I read the docs, ran small tests, and loaded real data to watch it work. I could have started coding day one, but our reports were already broken, so two weeks of learning meant I shipped it clean."

**Q: "What if you had not learned the 'small writes' thing first?"**
> "It would have failed in production within hours. The old code wrote one event at a time. That makes too many small files and breaks ClickHouse. Learning that first is exactly what saved us."

**Q: "Why did you choose the 1000 batch size?"**
> "I tuned it. I started at 100, but I saw 'too many parts' warnings, which means too many small files. So I raised it to 1000. That was big enough to be clean, but small enough that data did not wait too long. The docs also suggest 1000 and up."

**Q: "Why Go and not Python, which you already knew?"**
> "Go's channels were a clean fit for the buffer-and-flush pattern, and Go is lighter on memory. Python could do it, but Go was the right tool here. So I chose to learn it rather than force the easy path."

**Q: "How did you make sure there was no data loss?"**
> "The two-week dual-write. I wrote to both databases at once and checked the row counts and some sample profiles matched. Only after they matched did I switch the reads over and stop the old writes. So I never cut over on hope — I cut over on proof."

---

Ab tere paas teeno core stories full, simple-English, proper format me ready hain:
- **Bias for Action →** Spring Boot (W5) + Audit log silent failure (W1)
- **Learn & Be Curious →** ClickHouse (G1)

Bol, agla question le aa, ya kisi story ki aur practice karni ho.
